pull.title: cmake: compact generated raw and JSON C++ headers
pull.body:
Draft until I figure out the the CI failure on Windows:
> ClCompile target) -> D:\a\bitcoin\bitcoin\build\src\node\data\ip_asn.dat.h(45621,1): error C1060: compiler is out of heap space

----

### Problem

The current generated raw and JSON headers used in tests, benchmarks, and embedded node data are much larger and noisier than they need to be.

For example, `build/src/bench/data/block413567.raw.h` currently contains lines like:

```cpp
std::byte{0x04},std::byte{0x00},std::byte{0x00},std::byte{0x00},std::byte{0x11},std::byte{0xce},std::byte{0xc5},std::byte{0xc6},
```

That file alone is **15.38 MiB** across **124,994** lines before this change.

Likewise, `build/src/test/data/sighash.json.h` contains lines like:

```cpp
'\x5b','\x0a','\x09','\x5b','\x22','\x72','\x61','\x77',
```

That file is **1.43 MiB** across **26,319** lines before this change.


### Fix
This PR keeps the embedded data the same and only changes its C++ representation.

Across all generated raw and JSON headers touched by these two generators, total generated header size drops from **43.17 MiB** to **12.38 MiB** (**71.3%** smaller), and total line count drops from **396,633** to **99,585** (**74.9%** fewer lines).

### Structure

The first commit adds hash checks for the generated fixture headers that already have test coverage in `test_bitcoin` and `unitester`.
Those checks pin the current values before the representation changes.

The second commit compacts the generated JSON headers, emitting a concatenated byte string literals instead of one initializer per byte.

The third commit compacts the generated raw headers, emitting concatenated byte string literals instead of one `std::byte{0x..}` initializer per byte. It keeps a compact literal-backed `char[]` and exposes the existing byte span view with `MakeByteSpan`.
That keeps callers unchanged.

### Considerations

`_hex` was already investigated in https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/31861.
It was rejected because very large generated headers made that approach impractical for the compiler.

For raw headers, the final design avoids any compile-time byte materialization step.
That matters most for the large embedded `ip_asn.dat` blob.
The exported raw view becomes `const` instead of `constexpr` because `MakeByteSpan` is not `constexpr`.
The runtime behavior stays the same.

## Before and after

### Raw header sample

> Before:

```cpp
inline constexpr std::byte detail_block413567_raw[] {
std::byte{0x04},std::byte{0x00},std::byte{0x00},std::byte{0x00},std::byte{0x11},std::byte{0xce},std::byte{0xc5},std::byte{0xc6},
std::byte{0x5e},std::byte{0x00},std::byte{0xd3},std::byte{0x5b},std::byte{0x08},std::byte{0x86},std::byte{0x0e},std::byte{0x4e},
```

> After:

```cpp
inline const char detail_block413567_raw[] {
"\x04\x00\x00\x00\x11\xce\xc5\xc6\x5e\x00\xd3\x5b\x08\x86\x0e\x4e..."
```

### JSON header sample

> Before:

```cpp
inline constexpr char detail_sighash_bytes[] {
'\x5b','\x0a','\x09','\x5b','\x22','\x72','\x61','\x77',
'\x5f','\x74','\x72','\x61','\x6e','\x73','\x61','\x63',
```

> After:

```cpp
inline constexpr char detail_sighash_bytes[] {
"\x5b\x0a\x09\x5b\x22\x72\x61\x77\x5f\x74\x72\x61\x6e\x73\x61\x63..."
```

## Generated header sizes

<details>
<summary>Raw headers</summary>

| Header | Before size | Before lines | After size | After lines | Size reduction | Line reduction |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| `build/src/bench/data/block413567.raw.h` | 15.38 MiB | 124,994 | 3.90 MiB | 31,254 | 74.6% | 75.0% |
| `build/src/node/data/ip_asn.dat.h` | 23.37 MiB | 189,970 | 5.93 MiB | 47,498 | 74.6% | 75.0% |
| `build/src/test/data/asmap.raw.h` | 1.09 KiB | 16 | 430 B | 9 | 61.5% | 43.8% |
| **Total** | **38.75 MiB** | **314,980** | **9.84 MiB** | **78,761** | **74.6%** | **75.0%** |
</details>

verification.payload:
tree 6aaaf9b636f05db352593120f44a6d81f7aa9ac0
parent 7e3e22e1f7d6f46dad6b4df7ce37e301bc9a334f
author Lőrinc <pap.lorinc@gmail.com> 1776002097 +0200
committer Lőrinc <pap.lorinc@gmail.com> 1776020085 +0200

test: lock generated fixture hashes

The next commits change only how the generated headers are written in C++.
Lock the current fixture values first so those commits only need to change representation.

Cover both the `test_bitcoin` fixtures and the generated univalue JSON fixtures.
This keeps the later compaction commits focused on code generation while still proving that the embedded data did not change.

This keeps the later compaction commits focused on code generation while still proving that the embedded data did not change.

verification.payload:
tree b0bb2e4d078eccb0b416e8ae679d9729154c67b7
parent b37cd5eae817db6e935cbaee4f8ecc1e9bcd1e26
author Lőrinc <pap.lorinc@gmail.com> 1776015746 +0200
committer Lőrinc <pap.lorinc@gmail.com> 1776020140 +0200

cmake: compact generated JSON headers

The generated JSON headers currently emit one initializer per byte, making the generated source much larger than it needs to be.
Emit concatenated byte string literals instead and build the exported `std::string_view` directly from the array pointer and size.

events.message:
cmake: compact generated JSON headers

The generated JSON headers currently emit one initializer per byte, making the generated source much larger than it needs to be.
Emit concatenated byte string literals instead and build the exported `std::string_view` directly from the array pointer and size.

verification.payload:
tree 9ec2d9a104e43ab7dbda3453648bfb371b270d5f
parent 1602f471df174b1409a61167dd749fa6b3199c67
author Lőrinc <pap.lorinc@gmail.com> 1776015790 +0200
committer Lőrinc <pap.lorinc@gmail.com> 1776022883 +0200

cmake: compact generated raw headers

The generated raw headers currently emit one `std::byte{0x..}` initializer per byte.
That makes the generated source much larger than it needs to be.

Emit concatenated byte string literals instead and keep the generated storage as a compact literal-backed `char[]`.
This avoids the compile-time byte materialization that caused the Windows CI build to run out of compiler heap space.

A `_hex`-style approach was considered, but it pushes too much work into compile-time evaluation for large generated inputs.
The exported view becomes `const` instead of `constexpr` because `MakeByteSpan` is not `constexpr`.

Co-authored-by: Fabian Jahr <fjahr@protonmail.com>

events.message:
cmake: compact generated raw headers

The generated raw headers currently emit one `std::byte{0x..}` initializer per byte.
That makes the generated source much larger than it needs to be.

Emit concatenated byte string literals instead and keep the generated storage as a compact literal-backed `char[]`.
This avoids the compile-time byte materialization that caused the Windows CI build to run out of compiler heap space.

A `_hex`-style approach was considered, but it pushes too much work into compile-time evaluation for large generated inputs.
The exported view becomes `const` instead of `constexpr` because `MakeByteSpan` is not `constexpr`.

Co-authored-by: Fabian Jahr <fjahr@protonmail.com>
